options.feedback: Correct! East and West African trypanosomiasis (T. b. rhodesiense and T. b. gambiense) are caused when infective forms are introduced into the human body through the bite of the tsetse fly, not sand fleas.

options.text: Giardia lamblia—ingestion of water contaminated with cysts
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct match.

options.text: Hookworm—skin penetration of larvae from soil
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct match.

options.text: Toxoplasma gondii—ingestion of raw or rare meats
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct match.

question: Upon examination of stool material for Cystoisospora belli, one would expect to see:

options.feedback: Incorrect. C. belli produces oocysts, not cysts.
Incorrect. Chromatoidal bars are seen in Entamoeba cysts.

options.text: Oocysts that are modified acid-fast variable
options.feedback: Correct! C. belli oocysts in various stages of maturity would be seen in the concentration sediment or possibly the direct, wet preparation; these oocysts would stain positive with modified acid-fast stains.
Incorrect. Hematoxylin is not the primary stain for C. belli.

question: Which specimen is the least likely to provide recovery of Trichomonas vaginalis?

options.feedback: Incorrect. T. vaginalis can be recovered from urine.
Incorrect. T. vaginalis can be recovered from urethral discharge.
Incorrect. T. vaginalis can be recovered from vaginal discharge.
Correct! T. vaginalis is site specific. The organisms are found in the urogenital tract; thus, the intestinal tract is not the normal site for these organisms.

question: Which of the following is the best technique to identify Dientamoeba fragilis in stool?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Formalin concentrate is not the best for D. fragilis.
Correct! Because there is no known cyst form, the best technique to recover and identify D. fragilis trophozoites would be the trichrome-stained smear.
Incorrect. Modified acid-fast is for coccidia.
Incorrect. Giemsa's stain is not the primary stain for D. fragilis.

question: One of the following protozoan organisms has been implicated in waterborne and foodborne outbreaks within the United States. The suspect organism is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. P. hominis is not typically implicated in outbreaks.
Incorrect. D. fragilis is not typically waterborne.
Correct! For a number of years, G. lamblia has been implicated in both waterborne and foodborne outbreaks from the ingestion of infective cysts within contaminated water and food.
Incorrect. B. coli is associated with pigs and is not common in the United States.

question: A Gram stain from a gum lesion showed what appeared to be amoebae. A trichrome smear showed amoebae with a single nucleus and partially digested PMNs. The correct identification is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. T. tenax is a flagellate, not an amoeba.
Incorrect. E. histolytica does not typically ingest PMNs.

Correct! E. gingivalis is known to be an inhabitant of the mouth and is characterized by morphology that resembles Entamoeba histolytica/E. dispar. However, E. gingivalis tends to ingest PMNs, whereas Entamoeba histolytica/E. dispar do not.
Incorrect. E. polecki is not typically found in the mouth.

question: An Entamoeba histolytica trophozoite has the following characteristics:

options.text: Central karyosome in the nucleus, ingested RBCs, and clear pseudopodia
options.feedback: Correct! The trophozoite of E. histolytica has evenly arranged chromatin on the nuclear membrane; a central, compact karyosome in the nucleus; clear pseudopodia; and ingested RBCs in the cytoplasm.

options.text: Ingested RBCs, clear pseudopodia, and uneven chromatin on the nuclear membrane
options.feedback: Incorrect. The chromatin on the nuclear membrane is evenly arranged, not uneven.

options.text: Ingested RBCs, clear pseudopodia, and large glycogen vacuoles in cytoplasm
options.feedback: Incorrect. Glycogen vacuoles are seen in cysts, not trophozoites.

options.text: Large, blotlike karyosome, ingested white blood cells (WBCs), and granular pseudopods
options.feedback: Incorrect. The karyosome is central and compact, not blotlike, and WBCs are not ingested.

question: A 12-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department with meningitis and a history of swimming in a warm-water spring. Motile amoebae that measure 10 μ in size are seen in the CSF and are most likely:

options.feedback: Incorrect. I. bütschlii is not found in CSF.
Incorrect. E. nana is not found in CSF.
Incorrect. D. fragilis is not found in CSF.

Correct! N. fowleri are free-living soil and water amoebae that cause primary amoebic meningoencephalitis, or PAM. The number of cases reported is few; however, the infection is very acute and almost always fatal.

question: Characteristics of the rhabditiform (noninfective) larvae of Strongyloides stercoralis include a:

options.text: Short buccal capsule and large genital primordium
options.feedback: Correct! The rhabditiform larvae of S. stercoralis are characterized by the short buccal capsule (mouth) and large genital primordium, whereas hookworm larvae have a long buccal capsule and very small genital primordium.

options.text: Long buccal capsule and pointed tail
options.feedback: Incorrect. This describes hookworm larvae.

options.text: Short buccal capsule and small genital primordium
options.feedback: Incorrect. S. stercoralis has a large genital primordium.

options.text: Small genital primordium and notch in tail
options.feedback: Incorrect. This describes hookworm larvae.

question: Visceral larva migrans is associated with which of the following organisms?

options.feedback: Correct! Toxocara spp. are the cause of visceral larva migrans and occur when humans accidentally ingest the infective eggs of the dog or cat ascarid. The larvae migrate through the deep tissues, including the eye. The test of choice is the serology.
Incorrect. Onchocerca causes onchocerciasis, not visceral larva migrans.
Incorrect. Dracunculus causes dracunculiasis.
Incorrect. Angiostrongylus causes eosinophilic meningoencephalitis.

question: The following organisms are linked with specific, relevant information. The incorrect combination is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct match.
Incorrect. This is a correct match.
Incorrect. This is a correct match.

options.text: Balantidium coli—common within the United States
options.feedback: Correct! B. coli is a ciliate that can cause watery diarrhea in humans and is associated with pigs; however, it is not commonly found within the United States.

question: Examination of 24-hour unpreserved urine specimen is sometimes helpful in the recovery of:

options.feedback: Incorrect. While T. vaginalis can be found in urine, the primary specimen is not 24-hour unpreserved urine.

Correct! S. haematobium blood flukes reside in the veins over the bladder. When the eggs are passed from the body, they are often found in urine; egg viability can also be determined in unpreserved urine.
Incorrect. E. vermicularis eggs are recovered by Scotch tape preparation, not urine.
Incorrect. S. stercoralis larvae are recovered from stool, not urine.

question: The examination of sputum may be necessary to diagnose infection with:

options.feedback: Correct! P. westermani adult worms are found in the lung, and eggs may be coughed up in the sputum. Consequently, both sputum and stool (if the sputum containing the eggs is swallowed) are the recommended specimens for examination for the eggs.
Incorrect. T. spiralis is diagnosed by muscle biopsy, not sputum.
Incorrect. W. bancrofti is diagnosed by blood smears.
Incorrect. F. hepatica is diagnosed by stool examination.

question: Two helminth eggs that may resemble one another are:

options.feedback: Correct! Both D. latum and P. westermani eggs are operculated and approximately the same size. The morphology is similar, although D. latum has a knob at the abopercular end and P. westermani has a thickened abopercular end and shoulders into which the operculum fits.
Incorrect. These eggs have different morphology.
Incorrect. T. saginata eggs are striated and H. nana eggs have polar filaments.
Incorrect. These eggs have different morphology.

question: Eating poorly cooked pork can lead to an infection with:

options.feedback: Correct! Both T. solium (pork tapeworm) and T. spiralis can be acquired from the ingestion of raw or poorly cooked pork.
Incorrect. T. saginata is from beef, not pork.
Incorrect. T. trichiura is from soil, not pork.
Incorrect. D. latum is from fish, not pork.

question: An operculated cestode egg that can be recovered from human feces is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. C. sinensis is a trematode, not a cestode.
Correct! D. latum is the only operculated cestode egg that is found in humans; the infection is acquired from the ingestion of raw freshwater fish.
Incorrect. P. westermani is a trematode, not a cestode.
Incorrect. D. caninum eggs are not operculated.

question: The adult tapeworm of Echinococcus granulosus is found in the intestine of:

options.feedback: Correct! Although the hydatid cysts are found in sheep or in humans (accidental intermediate host), the adult tapeworms of E. granulosus are found in the intestine of the dog.
Incorrect. Sheep are intermediate hosts.
Incorrect. Humans are accidental intermediate hosts.
Incorrect. Cattle are not the definitive host.

question: In infections with Taenia solium, humans can serve as the:

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is one possibility, but not the only one.
Incorrect. This is one possibility, but not the only one.

options.text: Either the definitive or the intermediate host
options.feedback: Correct! If humans ingest T. solium cysticerci in uncooked or rare pork, the adult tapeworm will mature within the intestine (human will serve as definitive host); if eggs from the adult tapeworm are ingested, then the cysticerci will develop in human tissues (accidental intermediate host), causing cysticercosis.
Incorrect. Humans can serve as both.

question: Humans acquire infections with Diphyllobothrium latum adult worms by:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Freshwater crabs are not the source.
Incorrect. This is for schistosomiasis.
Incorrect. Water chestnuts are not the source.
Correct! The ingestion of raw freshwater fish containing the encysted larvae of D. latum will result in the development of an adult tapeworm within the human intestine.

question: Humans can serve as both the intermediate and definitive host in infections caused by:

options.feedback: Incorrect. E. vermicularis has a direct life cycle.

Correct! In infections with H. nana, humans serve as both intermediate and definitive hosts. When ingested, the oncosphere penetrates the intestinal mucosa, develops into the mature cysticercoid (human is intermediate host), and returns to the gut, where the adult tapeworm matures (human is definitive host).
Incorrect. S. japonicum has separate intermediate and definitive hosts.
Incorrect. A. lumbricoides has a direct life cycle.

question: Babesia has been implicated in disease from both splenectomized and nonsplenectomized patients. Morphologically, the parasites resemble:

options.feedback: Correct! Babesia is an intracellular parasite that closely resembles the ring forms (early trophozoites) of P. falciparum. Often in babesiosis, there are more rings per cell and the ring form is the only stage seen.
Incorrect. Leishmania amastigotes are different in morphology.
Incorrect. Trypomastigotes are different in morphology.
Incorrect. Microsporidial spores are different in morphology.

question: Organisms (and infections) that under normal conditions cannot be transmitted in the laboratory are:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Cryptosporidium can be transmitted in the laboratory.
Incorrect. T. solium can be transmitted in the laboratory.

Correct! A. lumbricoides eggs require a period of development in the soil before they are infective for humans. The other organisms listed can be transmitted within the laboratory or in the hospital setting.
Incorrect. E. vermicularis can be transmitted in the laboratory.

question: Toxoplasma gondii is characterized by:

options.text: Possible congenital infection and ingestion of oocysts
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is one characteristic, but not the only one.

options.text: Cosmopolitan distribution and possible difficulties with interpretation of serological results
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is one characteristic, but not the only one.
Incorrect. Both A and B are correct.

Correct! Infection with T. gondii is acquired through the ingestion of rare or raw meats, infective oocysts from cat feces, or as a congenital transmission. The organism has a cosmopolitan distribution and although serological testing is generally the test of choice, the results may be very difficult to interpret in certain situations.

question: Oocysts of Cryptosporidium spp. can be detected in stool specimens using:

options.feedback: Correct! The oocysts of Cryptosporidium spp. can be found and identified using microscopic examination of fecal smears stained with modified acid-fast stains. They appear as purple-red-pink round objects, measuring approximately 4–6 μ.
Incorrect. Cryptosporidium does not stain well with Gram stain.
Incorrect. Methenamine silver is used for fungi.
Incorrect. Trichrome is used for protozoan cysts and trophozoites, not Cryptosporidium.

question: Which microfilariae are usually not found circulating in the peripheral blood?

options.feedback: Incorrect. B. malayi microfilariae are found in peripheral blood.
Incorrect. W. bancrofti microfilariae are found in peripheral blood.
Correct! The microfilariae of O. volvulus are normally found in the fluid right under the outer layer of skin. Therefore, the skin snip is the proper specimen to examine.
Incorrect. L. loa microfilariae are found in peripheral blood.

question: Massive hemolysis, blackwater fever, and central nervous system involvement are most common with:

options.feedback: Incorrect. P. vivax does not typically cause massive hemolysis or blackwater fever.

Correct! The pathogenic sequelae of malarial infections with P. falciparum are the most severe of the five species of human malaria. They can include massive hemolysis, blackwater fever, and multiple organ involvement, including the central nervous system (cerebral malaria).
Incorrect. P. ovale does not typically cause severe complications.
Incorrect. P. malariae does not typically cause severe complications.

question: Organisms that should be considered in a nursery school outbreak of diarrhea include:

options.text: Endolimax nana, Giardia lamblia, and Entamoeba coli
options.feedback: Incorrect. E. nana and E. coli are nonpathogenic.

options.text: Giardia lamblia, Dientamoeba fragilis, and Cryptosporidium spp.
options.feedback: Correct! G. lamblia, D. fragilis, and Cryptosporidium have been implicated in nursery school outbreaks. Among the many protozoa and coccidia found in the human, these three organisms have become the most likely parasites in this type of setting.

options.text: Cryptosporidium spp., Trichomonas vaginalis, and Entamoeba coli
options.feedback: Incorrect. T. vaginalis is sexually transmitted and E. coli is nonpathogenic.

options.text: Pentatrichomonas hominis, Dientamoeba fragilis, and Endolimax nana
options.feedback: Incorrect. P. hominis and E. nana are nonpathogenic.

question: The incorrect match between disease and symptoms is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct match.
Incorrect. This is a correct match.

options.text: Toxoplasmosis in compromised host—central nervous system symptoms
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct match.

Correct! Infections with E. vermicularis (the pinworm) may cause anal itching, sleeplessness, and possibly some vaginal irritation or discharge; however, dysentery (bloody diarrhea) has not been associated with this infection.

question: The formalin–ether (ethyl acetate) concentration procedure for feces is used to demonstrate:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Motility is demonstrated on direct wet film.

Correct! The ova and parasite examination contains three components: the direct wet film (demonstrates protozoan trophozoite motility), the formalin–ethyl acetate concentration (demonstrates protozoan cysts, coccidian oocysts, and helminth eggs), and the trichrome or iron hematoxylin–stained smear (confirms protozoan cysts and trophozoites).
Incorrect. Pseudopod formation is observed on direct wet film.
Incorrect. Trophozoites are best observed on direct wet film or stained smear.

question: Cysts of Iodamoeba bütschlii typically have:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Chromatoidal bars are seen in other Entamoeba species.
Incorrect. This is not characteristic of I. bütschlii.
Correct! The cyst of I. bütschlii is characterized by a large glycogen vacuole that is seen on the wet smear (stains brown with iodine) and on the permanent stained smear (vacuole will appear clear).

options.text: Many ingested bacteria and yeast cells
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is seen in trophozoites, not cysts.

question: The miracidial hatching test helps to demonstrate the viability of eggs of:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Taenia eggs do not hatch via miracidia.

Correct! The determination of egg viability is important in schistosomiasis; therefore, the miracidial hatching test is helpful in demonstrating the egg viability of Schistosoma species. Once the eggs are hatched, the living miracidium larvae will be visible in the water.
Incorrect. Hookworm eggs do not hatch via miracidia.
Incorrect. Opisthorchis eggs do not hatch via miracidia.

question: Organisms that should be considered in a waterborne outbreak of diarrheal disease include:

options.feedback: Correct! Both G. lamblia and Cryptosporidium have been implicated in waterborne outbreaks of diarrheal disease. These infections would result from the ingestion of G. lamblia cysts and/or Cryptosporidium oocysts.
Incorrect. E. nana is nonpathogenic.
Incorrect. B. hominis is controversial and T. vaginalis is sexually transmitted.
Incorrect. T. gondii is not typically waterborne and S. mansoni requires a snail intermediate host.

question: Fecal immunoassays have become more commonly used to diagnose infections with:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Immunoassays are not commonly used for these organisms.

Correct! Rapid fecal immunoassays have become more widely used for the diagnosis of infections with G. lamblia and Cryptosporidium. For these two organisms, the fecal immunoassays are more sensitive than the routine O&P examination.
Incorrect. Immunoassays are not commonly used for helminth infections.
Incorrect. T. vaginalis is diagnosed by wet mount, not fecal immunoassay.

question: Primary infections with the microsporidia may originate in:

options.feedback: Incorrect. The primary site is the gastrointestinal tract.
Incorrect. The primary site is the gastrointestinal tract.

Correct! With the possible exception of direct inoculation infection in the eye, the microsporidia are thought to initially infect the gastrointestinal (GI) tract through ingestion of the infective spores; infections in other body sites are thought to disseminate from the GI tract.
Incorrect. The primary site is the gastrointestinal tract.

question: Eye infections with Acanthamoeba spp. have most commonly been traced to:

options.feedback: Incorrect. The primary cause is contaminated lens care solutions.
Incorrect. The primary cause is contaminated lens care solutions.

options.text: Use of contaminated lens care solutions
options.feedback: Correct! The majority of eye infections with Acanthamoeba spp. have resulted from the use of contaminated eye care solutions, primarily the use of homemade saline. It is recommended that all solutions be discarded at the expiration date.

options.text: Failure to remove lenses while swimming
options.feedback: Incorrect. While this is a risk factor, the primary cause is contaminated lens care solutions.

question: Select the most sensitive recovery method for Acanthamoeba spp. from lens care solutions or corneal biopsies.

options.feedback: Incorrect. Trichrome is not the most sensitive method.

options.text: The use of monoclonal reagents for the detection of antibody
options.feedback: Incorrect. Immunoassays are not the most sensitive recovery method.

options.text: The use of non-nutrient agar cultures seeded with Escherichia coli
options.feedback: Correct! Currently, the most sensitive method for the recovery of Acanthamoeba spp. from clinical specimens is the non-nutrient agar culture seeded with E. coli. The amoebae feed on the bacteria; both trophozoites and cysts can be recovered from the agar surface.
Incorrect. Giemsa's stain is not the most sensitive method.

question: The microsporidia are protozoans (now classified with the fungi) that have been implicated in human disease primarily in:

options.feedback: Correct! Although the microsporidia have been known as pathogens in many groups of animals, their involvement in humans has primarily been in immunocompromised patients, especially those with AIDS. Microsporidia can be found in different tissues, and currently there are approximately 10 genera implicated in human disease.

options.text: Pediatric patients under the age of 5 years
options.feedback: Incorrect. Immunocompromised patients are the primary group.
Incorrect. Immunocompromised patients are the primary group.

options.text: Patients who have been traveling in the tropics
options.feedback: Incorrect. Immunocompromised patients are the primary group.

question: When staining Cystoisospora belli oocysts with modified acid-fast stains, the important difference between these methods and the acid-fast stains used for acid-fast bacilli (AFB) is:

options.text: The staining time is much longer with regular AFB acid-fast stains
options.feedback: Incorrect. The time difference is not the primary distinction.

options.text: The decolorizer is weaker than acid alcohol used for AFB decolorizing
options.feedback: Correct! The decolorizer in modified acid-fast stains (Kinyoun's cold method, modified hot method) is usually 1% sulfuric acid rather than the stronger acid alcohol used in the routine AFB stains.

options.text: A counterstain must be used for the modified methods
options.feedback: Incorrect. Counterstain is used in both methods.

options.text: The stain is more concentrated when staining for AFB
options.feedback: Incorrect. The concentration difference is not the primary distinction.

question: The incorrect match between symptoms and disease is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct match.
Incorrect. This is a correct match.
Incorrect. This is a correct match.

Correct! T. trichiura (whipworm) may cause diarrhea and occasionally dysentery in very heavy infections; however, the worms are confined to the intestine, and myalgias are not seen in this helminth infection.

question: The incorrect match between organism and characteristic is:

options.text: Chilomastix mesnili—Shepherd's crook and lemon shape
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct match.
Incorrect. This is a correct match.

Correct! H. nana has a thin eggshell containing a six-hooked embryo (oncosphere) and polar filaments that lie between the eggshell and the embryo. The striated eggshell is generally associated with Taenia spp. eggs.
Incorrect. This is a correct match.

question: The incorrect match between method and method objective is:

options.text: Direct wet examination—detection of organism motility
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct match.

options.text: Knott's concentration—the recovery of operculated helminth eggs
options.feedback: Correct! The Knott concentration is designed to allow the recovery of microfilariae from a blood specimen. Dilute formalin (2%) is used; blood is introduced into the formalin, the red cells lyse, and the sediment can be examined as a wet preparation or permanent stained smear for the presence of microfilariae.
Incorrect. This is a correct match.

options.text: Permanent stained fecal smear—confirmation of protozoa
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is a correct match.

question: The incorrect match between organism and characteristic is:

options.text: Dientamoeba fragilis—tetrad karyosome in the nucleus
options.feedback: Correct! D. fragilis has chromatin that is fragmented into two to four masses, but they are not arranged as a tetrad karyosome. This is a description that may be confused with other organisms.
Incorrect. This is a correct match.
Incorrect. This is a correct match.
Incorrect. This is a correct match.

question: There are few procedures considered STAT in parasitology. The most obvious situation would be:

options.text: Ova and parasite examination for giardiasis
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is not typically STAT.
Incorrect. This is not typically STAT.

Correct! The request for blood films for malaria should always be considered a stat request. Any laboratory providing these services should be available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. In cases of P. falciparum malaria, any delay in diagnosing the infection could be fatal for the patient.
Incorrect. This is not typically STAT.

question: An immunosuppressed man has several episodes of pneumonia, intestinal pain, sepsis with gram-negative rods, and a history of military service in Southeast Asia 20 years earlier. The most likely cause is infection with:

options.feedback: Incorrect. T. cruzi is associated with Central and South America, not Southeast Asia.

Correct! A latent infection with S. stercoralis acquired years before may cause severe symptoms in the immunosuppressed patient ('autoinfective' capability of life cycle and migratory route of the larvae through the body).
Incorrect. N. fowleri causes acute meningoencephalitis, not chronic infection.
Incorrect. P. westermani causes lung infection, not sepsis.

question: In a pediatric patient, the recommended clinical specimen for recovery of Enterobius vermicularis is the:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Stool specimens are not the best for E. vermicularis.
Incorrect. This is not the recommended method.
Incorrect. This is not the recommended method.
Correct! In a pediatric patient, the most appropriate diagnostic test for recovery of Enterobius vermicularis eggs is a series of Scotch tape preparations.

question: Which parasite causes eosinophilic meningoencephalitis, a form of larva migrans causing fever, headache, stiff neck, and increased cells in the spinal fluid?

options.feedback: Incorrect. N. americanus causes hookworm disease, not meningoencephalitis.

Correct! Eosinophilic meningoencephalitis is a form of larva migrans and is caused by A. cantonensis, the rat lungworm. This Pacific area infection is associated with CSF symptoms and sometimes eye involvement.
Incorrect. A. braziliense causes cutaneous larva migrans.
Incorrect. S. stercoralis causes strongyloidiasis, not eosinophilic meningoencephalitis.

question: 'Cultures of parasites are different from bacterial cultures; no quality control is needed.' This statement is:

options.text: True, if two tubes of media are set up for each patient
options.feedback: Incorrect. This does not eliminate the need for quality control.

options.text: True, if the media are checked every 24 hours
options.feedback: Incorrect. This does not eliminate the need for quality control.

options.text: False, unless two different types of media are used
options.feedback: Incorrect. Quality control is needed regardless.

options.text: False, and organism and media controls need to be set up
options.feedback: Correct! Duplicate cultures should be set up, and specific American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) strains should be cultured along with the patient specimens to confirm that the culture system is operating properly.

question: Protozoan cysts were seen in a concentration sediment and tentatively identified as Entamoeba coli. However, the organisms were barely visible on the permanent stained smear because:

options.text: The organisms were actually not present in the concentrate sediment
options.feedback: Incorrect. They were visible in the concentrate.

options.text: There were too few cysts to allow identification on the stained smear
options.feedback: Incorrect. The issue is not quantity.

options.text: E. coli cysts were present but poorly fixed
options.feedback: Correct! As E. coli cysts mature, the cyst wall becomes more impenetrable to fixatives. Consequently, the cysts may be visible in the concentrate sediment but appear very distorted or pale on the permanent stained smear.

options.text: The concentrate and permanent stained smear were not from the same patient
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is not the likely explanation.

question: When humans have hydatid disease, the causative agent and host classification are:

options.feedback: Correct! The cause of hydatid disease is E. granulosus, and the human is classified as the accidental intermediate host. Infection occurs when humans accidentally ingest the eggs of E. granulosus and the hydatid cysts develop in the liver, lung, and other organs.
Incorrect. Humans are accidental intermediate hosts, not definitive hosts.
Incorrect. T. solium causes cysticercosis, not hydatid disease.
Incorrect. T. solium causes cysticercosis, not hydatid disease.

question: A 45-year-old hunter developed fever, myalgia, and periorbital edema. He has a history of bear meat consumption. The most likely causative agent is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. T. gondii does not typically present with periorbital edema.
Incorrect. T. solium does not typically present with these symptoms.
Incorrect. H. nana does not typically present with these symptoms.
Correct! Bear meat is another excellent source of T. spiralis. In this case, the patient had evidently consumed poorly cooked bear meat, thus ingesting the encysted larvae of T. spiralis.

question: In a condition resulting from the accidental ingestion of eggs, the human becomes the intermediate rather than the definitive host. The correct answer is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Trichinosis results from ingestion of encysted larvae in meat.

Correct! The accidental ingestion of T. solium eggs can result in the disease called cysticercosis. The cysticerci will develop in a number of different tissues, including the brain, and the human is the accidental intermediate host.
Incorrect. Ascariasis results from ingestion of eggs but humans are definitive hosts.
Incorrect. Strongyloidiasis involves autoinfection, not accidental egg ingestion.

question: A transplant patient on immunosuppressive drugs developed increasing diarrhea. The most likely combination of disease and diagnostic procedure is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Trichinosis does not present with diarrhea.

Correct! The fact that the patient has received a transplant, is on immunosuppressive drugs, and has continuing diarrhea suggests microsporidiosis; the appropriate diagnostic test would be modified trichrome staining of fecal material.
Incorrect. Toxoplasmosis does not typically present with diarrhea.
Incorrect. Paragonimiasis presents with hemoptysis, not diarrhea.

question: After returning from a 2-year stay in India, a patient has eosinophilia, an enlarged left spermatic cord, and bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy. The most likely clinical specimen and organism match is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Leishmania is found in macrophages, not blood films.
Incorrect. T. vaginalis does not cause these symptoms.
Incorrect. Babesia is found in blood but does not cause these symptoms.

Correct! Based on the history, the most relevant procedure to perform is the preparation and examination of thick blood films for the recovery and identification of microfilariae. The symptoms suggest early filariasis.

question: Patients with severe diarrhea should use 'enteric precautions' to prevent nosocomial infections with:

options.feedback: Incorrect. G. lamblia is not typically a nosocomial concern.
Incorrect. A. lumbricoides is not typically a nosocomial concern.
Correct! Cryptosporidium oocysts are immediately infective when passed in stool, and nosocomial infections have been well documented with this coccidian.
Incorrect. C. belli oocysts are not immediately infective.

question: A 60-year-old Brazilian patient with cardiac irregularities and congestive heart failure suddenly dies. Examination of the myocardium revealed numerous amastigotes, an indication that the cause of death was most likely:

options.feedback: Incorrect. L. donovani affects the reticuloendothelial system, not cardiac muscle.
Incorrect. L. braziliense causes cutaneous lesions.
Incorrect. T. gambiense causes African sleeping sickness, not cardiac involvement.

Correct! T. cruzi, the cause of Chagas disease, has two forms within the human: the trypomastigote in the blood and the amastigote in the striated muscle (usually cardiac muscle and intestinal tract muscle).

question: When malaria smears are requested, what patient information should be obtained?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Diet is not relevant.
Incorrect. Sex is not relevant.

options.text: Travel history, antimalarial medication, date of return to United States
options.feedback: Correct! Travel history (areas of drug resistance), the date of return to the United States (primary versus relapse case), and history of antimalarial medication and illness are very important questions to ask. Without this information, a malaria diagnosis can be missed or delayed.
Incorrect. Diet is not relevant.

question: In an outbreak of diarrheal disease traced to a municipal water supply, the most likely causative agent is:

options.feedback: Correct! Cryptosporidium oocysts have been transmitted through contaminated municipal water supplies. Such outbreaks have been well documented.
Incorrect. C. belli is not typically waterborne.
Incorrect. E. histolytica is not typically waterborne in municipal supplies.
Incorrect. D. fragilis is not typically waterborne.

question: Within the United States, sporadic mini-outbreaks of diarrheal disease have been associated with the ingestion of strawberries, raspberries, fresh basil, mesclun (baby lettuce leaves), and snow peas. The most likely causative agent is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. D. fragilis is not associated with produce outbreaks.

Correct! The coccidian C. cayetanensis has been linked to mini-outbreaks of diarrheal disease. Epidemiological evidence strongly implicates various berries, basil, mesclun, and snow peas as likely causes.
Incorrect. S. mansoni is not transmitted through produce.
Incorrect. C. belli is not typically associated with produce outbreaks.

question: Which of the following statements is true regarding onchocerciasis?

options.text: The adult worm is present in the blood
options.feedback: Incorrect. The adult worm resides in subcutaneous nodules.

options.text: The microfilariae are in the blood during the late evening hours
options.feedback: Incorrect. This describes Wuchereria bancrofti.

options.text: The diagnostic test of choice is the skin snip
options.feedback: Correct! The adult O. volvulus reside in subcutaneous nodules, and the microfilariae are found in the fluids right under the outer layers of skin; thus, the appropriate diagnostic test is the microscopic examination of skin snips for the presence of microfilariae.

options.text: The parasite resides in the deep lymphatics
options.feedback: Incorrect. This describes Wuchereria bancrofti.

question: The most prevalent helminth to infect humans is:

options.feedback: Correct! The pinworm, E. vermicularis, is the most common parasitic helminth infection throughout the world, and the eggs are infective within just a few hours.

options.text: Ascaris lumbricoides, the large intestinal roundworm
options.feedback: Incorrect. While common, it is not the most prevalent.
Incorrect. T. saginata is less common than pinworm.

options.text: Schistosoma mansoni, one of the blood flukes
options.feedback: Incorrect. S. mansoni is less common than pinworm.

question: A helminth egg is described as having terminal polar plugs. The most likely helminth is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Hookworm eggs have thin shells and no polar plugs.
Correct! The eggs of T. trichiura (the whipworm) have been described as being barrel shaped with a thick shell and two polar plugs.
Incorrect. F. hepatica eggs are operculated.
Incorrect. D. latum eggs are operculated.

question: Ingestion of which of the following eggs will result in infection?

options.feedback: Incorrect. S. stercoralis eggs do not survive outside the body; rhabditiform larvae are the infective stage.
Incorrect. S. japonicum eggs require a snail intermediate host and do not cause infection by ingestion.

Correct! The eggs of T. canis are infectious for humans and cause visceral larva migrans. These ascarid eggs of the dog can infect humans; the eggs hatch and the larvae wander through the deep tissues, occasionally the eye.
Incorrect. O. sinensis eggs require a snail and fish intermediate host.

question: Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium ovale are similar because they:

options.text: Exhibit Schüffner's dots and have a true relapse in the life cycle
options.feedback: Correct! Both P. vivax and P. ovale infect young red cells, have true stippling (Schüffner's dots), contain malarial pigment, and have a true relapse stage in the life cycle.

options.text: Have no malarial pigment and multiple rings
options.feedback: Incorrect. Both have malarial pigment.

options.text: Commonly have appliqué forms in the red cells
options.feedback: Incorrect. Appliqué forms are characteristic of P. falciparum.

options.text: Have true stippling, do not have a relapse stage, and infect old red cells
options.feedback: Incorrect. Both have a relapse stage and infect young red cells.

question: The term internal autoinfection can be associated with the following parasites:

options.feedback: Incorrect. G. lamblia does not have internal autoinfection.
Incorrect. C. belli does not have internal autoinfection.

Correct! Both Cryptosporidium and S. stercoralis have an internal autoinfection capability in their life cycles. This means that the cycle and infection can continue even after the patient has left the endemic area.
Incorrect. Neither has internal autoinfection.

question: Microsporidia have been identified as causing severe diarrhea, disseminated disease in other body sites, and ocular infections. Routes of infection have been identified as:

options.feedback: Incorrect. This is one route, but not the only one.
Incorrect. This is one route, but not the only one.
Incorrect. This is one route, but not the only one.

Correct! Infectious routes for microsporidial infections have been confirmed as ingestion and inhalation of the spores; direct transfer of infectious spores from environmental surfaces to the eyes has also been reported.

question: An immunocompromised patient continues to have diarrhea after repeated ova and parasites (O&P) examinations (sedimentation concentration, trichrome permanent stained smear) were reported as negative; organisms that might be responsible for the diarrhea include:

options.text: Cryptosporidium spp., Giardia lamblia, and Cystoisospora belli
options.feedback: Incorrect. G. lamblia is detected by routine O&P.

options.text: Giardia lamblia, microsporidia, and Endolimax nana
options.feedback: Incorrect. G. lamblia and E. nana are detected by routine O&P.
Incorrect. Both are detected by routine O&P.

Correct! Routine O&P examinations usually do not allow the detection of Cryptosporidium spp. oocysts and microsporidial spores; special stains are required. Modified acid-fast stains for coccidia and modified trichrome stains for the microsporidial spores are recommended.

question: Confirmation of an infection with microsporidia can be achieved by seeing:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Microsporidia produce spores, not oocysts.
Incorrect. Sporozoites are not visible in microsporidial spores.
Correct! Confirmation of an infection with microsporidial spores can be achieved by seeing evidence of the polar tubule within the spores (horizontal or diagonal line across/within the spore).

options.text: Organisms stained with modified acid-fast stains
options.feedback: Incorrect. Microsporidia are stained with modified trichrome stains, not acid-fast.

question: Early ring stages of the fifth human malaria, Plasmodium knowlesi, resemble those of:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Early rings of P. knowlesi resemble P. falciparum.
Incorrect. Early rings of P. knowlesi resemble P. falciparum.
Correct! The early ring stages of Plasmodium knowlesi resemble those of P. falciparum.
Incorrect. Early rings of P. knowlesi resemble P. falciparum.

question: Parasite stages that are immediately infective for humans on passage from the gastrointestinal tract include:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Schistosoma eggs require a water environment to hatch.
Incorrect. Bradyzoites are infective but not immediately upon passage.
Incorrect. Trophozoites are not immediately infective.
Correct! Cryptosporidium spp. oocysts are immediately infectious when passed in the stool, regardless of the stool consistency.

question: Older developing stages (trophs, schizonts) of the fifth human malaria, Plasmodium knowlesi, resemble those of:

options.feedback: Correct! The older developmental stages of Plasmodium knowlesi (trophs, schizonts) resemble those seen in infections with P. malariae (band forms, rosette schizonts).
Incorrect. Older stages of P. knowlesi resemble P. malariae.
Incorrect. Older stages of P. knowlesi resemble P. malariae.
Incorrect. Older stages of P. knowlesi resemble P. malariae.

question: Autofluorescence requires no stain and is recommended for the identification of:

options.feedback: Incorrect. E. histolytica cysts do not autofluoresce.
Incorrect. T. gondii does not autofluoresce.
Incorrect. D. fragilis does not autofluoresce.
Correct! Autofluorescence requires no stain and is often recommended for confirmation of Cyclospora cayetanensis oocysts.

question: Key characteristics of infection with Plasmodium knowlesi include:

options.text: Rapid erythrocytic cycle (24 hr), will infect all ages of RBCs, and can cause serious disease
options.feedback: Correct! Key characteristics of an infection with Plasmodium knowlesi include a rapid life cycle (24 hr), the potential to infect all ages of RBCs, and the possibility of serious symptoms similar to those seen with P. falciparum infections. There is no relapse from the liver with this species.

options.text: Erythrocytic cycle limited to young RBCs and causes a relatively benign disease
options.feedback: Incorrect. P. knowlesi infects all ages of RBCs and can cause serious disease.

options.text: The possibility of a true relapse from the liver, infection in older RBCs, and causes serious disease
options.feedback: Incorrect. P. knowlesi does not have a true relapse from the liver.

options.text: Extended life cycle (72 hr), will infect all ages of RBCs, and disease is similar to that caused by P. ovale
options.feedback: Incorrect. The life cycle is 24 hours, not 72 hours.

question: Microsporidial infections can be confirmed using:

options.text: Light microscopy and modified trichrome stains
options.feedback: Correct! An infection with microsporidia can be confirmed using modified trichrome stains (10X the normal dye content found in routine trichrome stains) and light microscopy. The internal polar tubule will be visible within some of the spores.

options.text: Phase contrast microscopy and routine trichrome stains
options.feedback: Incorrect. Routine trichrome stains do not adequately stain microsporidia.

options.text: Electron microscopy and modified acid-fast stains
options.feedback: Incorrect. EM is not routinely used and acid-fast stains do not stain microsporidia.
Incorrect. Hematoxylin is not used for microsporidia.

question: Although the pathogenicity of Blastocystis hominis remains controversial, newer information suggests that:

options.text: Most organisms are misdiagnosed as artifacts
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is not the current understanding.

options.text: Numerous strains/species are included in the name, some of which are pathogenic and some are nonpathogenic
options.feedback: Correct! The name Blastocystis hominis contains approximately 10 different strains/species that are morphologically identical; some are pathogenic and some are nonpathogenic. Because we cannot separate these organisms by morphology, this probably explains why some patients are symptomatic and some remain asymptomatic.

options.text: The immune status of the host is solely responsible for symptomatic infections
options.feedback: Incorrect. While immune status plays a role, it is not the sole factor.

options.text: The number of organisms present determines pathogenicity
options.feedback: Incorrect. The strain type is more important than the number.

question: Potential problems using EDTA anticoagulant and holding the blood too long prior to preparation of thick and thin blood films include:

options.text: Changes in parasite morphology, loss of organisms within several hours, and poor staining
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is one set of problems, but not the only one.

options.text: Loss of Schüffner's dots, poor adherence of the blood to the glass slide, and parasites beginning the vector cycle within the tube of blood
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is one set of problems, but not the only one.
Incorrect. Both A and B are correct.

Correct! There are many changes that occur if blood is held longer than 1–2 hours prior to thick and thin blood film preparation. These include changes in parasite morphology, loss of parasites, poor staining, poor adherence of the blood to the slide, and the parasites beginning that portion of the life cycle that normally occurs within the vector.

question: Which of the following is a key characteristic of the thick blood film?

options.text: The ability to see the parasite within the RBCs
options.feedback: Incorrect. RBCs are lysed in the thick film.

options.text: The ability to identify the parasites to the species level
options.feedback: Incorrect. It is difficult to identify the parasite to the species level from the thick blood film.

options.text: The examination of less blood than the thin blood film
options.feedback: Incorrect. The thick film contains more blood than the thin film.

options.text: The necessity to lake the RBCs during or prior to staining
options.feedback: Correct! With the thick blood film, there is more blood per slide than on the thin film, resulting in greater sensitivity. The RBCs are 'laked' while drying or during staining, making it impossible to observe the parasites within the RBCs.

question: An emergency department physician ordered a culture and sensitivity test on a catheterized urine specimen obtained from a 24-year-old female patient. A colony count was done and gave the following results after 24 hours: Blood agar plate = >100,000 col/mL of gram-positive cocci resembling staphylococci; MacConkey agar = No growth; CNA plate = Inhibited growth; Hemolysis = Neg; Novobiocin = Resistant; Catalase = Positive. This isolate is:

options.feedback: Correct! CNA inhibits most strains of S. saprophyticus. Therefore, blood agar should be used when culturing catheterized urine samples from young female patients. Most S. saprophyticus isolates are obtained from female patients 20–30 years old.
Incorrect. Micrococcus does not typically cause UTIs.
Incorrect. S. aureus is coagulase positive and would likely be hemolytic.
Incorrect. S. pyogenes is catalase negative.

question: An outbreak of Staphylococcus aureus in the nursery department prompted the Infection Control Committee to proceed with an environmental screening procedure. The best screening media to use for this purpose would be:

options.feedback: Incorrect. CNA agar is not selective for S. aureus specifically.
Incorrect. THIO broth is not selective.

Correct! The high concentration of NaCl (7.5%) in mannitol salt agar allows for the recovery of S. aureus from heavily contaminated specimens while inhibiting other organisms. Also, S. aureus ferments mannitol, thus allowing for easy detection of yellow-haloed colonies on red mannitol salt agar.
Incorrect. PEA agar is for gram-positive organisms but not specific for S. aureus.

question: A listless 12-month-old boy with a fever of 103°F was taken to the emergency department. He had been diagnosed with an ear infection 3 days earlier. A spinal tap was performed, but only one tube of CSF was obtained from the lumbar puncture. The single tube of CSF should be submitted first to which department?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Microbiology should be prioritized to ensure pathogen recovery.

Correct! Generally, tube 2 or 3 is submitted to the microbiology laboratory for culture and Gram stain smear. To ensure recovery of any pathogens and correct diagnosis without other bacterial contamination, immediate centrifugation and inoculation to the appropriate media as well as a Gram stain smear should be performed prior to delivery of the specimen to the chemistry department for testing.
Incorrect. Hematology should not be prioritized over microbiology.
Incorrect. Cytology should not be prioritized over microbiology.

question: A 65-year-old female outpatient was requested by her physician to submit a 24-hour urine specimen for protein and creatinine tests. He also requested testing for mycobacteria in the urine. Should the microbiology laboratory accept this 24-hour specimen for culture?

options.text: Yes, if the specimen is kept on ice
options.feedback: Incorrect. A 24-hour urine is unsuitable for culture regardless of temperature.

options.text: Yes, if the specimen is for aerobic culture only
options.feedback: Incorrect. A 24-hour urine is unsuitable for culture.

options.text: No, the specimen must be kept at room temperature
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is not the reason for rejection.

options.text: No, the specimen is unsuitable for the recovery of mycobacteria
options.feedback: Correct! In general, a 24-hour urine is unsuitable for culture; a first morning specimen is best for the recovery of mycobacteria in the urine.

question: A lymph node biopsy obtained from a 30-year-old male patient was submitted to the microbiology laboratory for a culture and AFB smear for mycobacteria. The specimen was fixed in formalin. This specimen should be:

options.text: Accepted for AFB smear and cultured
options.feedback: Incorrect. Specimens submitted for culture should not be fixed.
Correct! Specimens submitted for culture and recovery of any bacteria should be submitted without fixatives.

options.text: Held at room temperature for 24 hours and then cultured
options.feedback: Incorrect. Formalin fixation is irreversible for culture.
Incorrect. Formalin makes the specimen unsuitable for any culture.

question: A 49-year-old man who traveled to Mexico City returned with a bad case of dysentery. His symptoms were fever; abdominal cramping; and bloody, mucoidal, frequent stools. In addition to this, many WBCs were seen on the Gram stain smear. Stool culture gave the following results: Gram stain: Gram-negative rods; Lactose = +; Indole = +; Lysine decarboxylase = Neg; Urease = Neg; Motility = Neg. What is the most likely organism?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Salmonella is usually lactose negative and motile.
Incorrect. Proteus is urease positive and motile.
Incorrect. E. coli is usually motile and lysine decarboxylase positive.

Correct! EIEC, or enteroinvasive E. coli, produces dysentery similar to that of Shigella, with invasion and destruction of the intestinal mucosal epithelium. Leukocytes are seen on the Gram stain smear. Adults who are travelers to foreign countries, especially Mexico, are at greatest risk.

question: An 80-year-old male patient was admitted to the hospital with a fever of 102°F. A sputum culture revealed many gram-negative rods on MacConkey agar and blood agar. The patient was diagnosed with pneumonia. The following biochemical results were obtained from the culture: H2S = Neg; Citrate = +; Motility = Neg; Lactose = +; Indole = +; Resistance to ampicillin and carbenicillin; Urease = +; VP = +. What is the most likely identification?

options.feedback: Correct! K. oxytoca is similar to K. pneumoniae except that the indole test is positive for K. oxytoca.
Incorrect. P. mirabilis is lactose negative and motile.
Incorrect. E. coli is VP negative and citrate negative.
Incorrect. K. pneumoniae is indole negative.

question: An immunocompromised 58-year-old female chemotherapy patient received 2 units of packed RBCs. The patient died 3 days later, and the report from the autopsy revealed that her death was due to septic shock. The blood bags were cultured, and the following results were noted: GROWTH OF AEROBIC GRAM-NEGATIVE RODS ON BOTH MACCONKEY AND BLOOD AGARS; Lactose = Neg; Sucrose = +; Indole = Neg; Urease = +; VP = Neg; Citrate = Neg; H2S = Neg; Motility 22°C = +; Motility 37°C = Neg. What is the most likely identification?

options.feedback: Incorrect. E. coli is lactose positive and motile at 37°C.
Correct! Y. enterocolitica has been associated with fatal bacteremia and septic shock from contaminated blood transfusion products. The motility at room temperature is a clue to this identification.
Incorrect. E. cloacae is motile at 37°C.
Incorrect. C. freundii is typically H2S positive and motile at 37°C.

question: A pediatric patient with severe bloody diarrhea who had been camping with his parents was admitted to the hospital with complications of hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS). Several stool specimens were cultured with the following results noted: Gram stain smear = Many gram-negative rods with no WBCs seen; Blood agar = Normal flora; MacConkey agar = Normal flora; MacConkey agar with sorbitol = Many clear colonies (sorbitol negative); Hektoen agar = Normal flora; Campy agar = No growth. What is the most likely identification?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Yersinia does not typically present with HUS.

Correct! E. coli O157:H7 is usually the most common isolate from bloody stools of the enterohemorrhagic E. coli (EHEC) group, which results from undercooked beef. These strains are waterborne and foodborne, and the infections from E. coli O157:H7 are greatest during the summer months in temperate climates.
Incorrect. Salmonella does not typically cause HUS.
Incorrect. Shigella does not typically cause HUS.

question: A 14-year-old emergency department patient had been to the doctor's office 2 days previously with abdominal pain, diarrhea, and a low-grade fever. He was diagnosed with pseudoappendicular syndrome. Cultures from the stool containing blood and WBCs showed the following results: AEROBIC GRAM-NEGATIVE RODS ON MACCONKEY AGAR (CLEAR COLONIES); Campy agar = No growth; Lactose = Neg; Sucrose = +; Indole = Neg; VP = Neg; Citrate = Neg; H2S = Neg; Motility 37°C = Neg; Motility 22°C = +; Hektoen agar = Normal flora. What is the most likely identification?

options.feedback: Correct! Y. enterocolitica is responsible for diseases in younger persons. Blood and leukocytes can be present in stools. Patients (usually teens) exhibiting appendicitis-like symptoms with lactose-negative colonies growing on MacConkey agar should be tested for the growth of Y. enterocolitica.
Incorrect. Salmonella is motile at 37°C and often H2S positive.
Incorrect. Shigella is nonmotile at both temperatures.
Incorrect. E. coli is motile at 37°C.

question: A sputum culture from a 13-year-old cystic fibrosis patient grew a predominance of short, gram-negative rods that tested oxidase negative. On MacConkey, chocolate, and blood agar plates, the organism appeared to have a lavender-green pigment. Further testing showed: Motility = +; Glucose = + (oxidative); Lysine decarboxylase = +; DNase = +; Maltose = + (oxidative); Esculin hydrolysis = +. What is the most likely identification?

options.feedback: Correct! S. maltophilia is the third most frequently isolated nonfermentative gram-negative rod in the clinical laboratory. Cystic fibrosis patients are at greater risk for infections because of previous antimicrobial treatment and recurrent pneumonia and because some strains may be colonizers.
Incorrect. Acinetobacter is oxidase negative but does not produce lavender-green pigment.
Incorrect. P. aeruginosa is oxidase positive.
Incorrect. B. cepacia is oxidase positive (weakly) and does not produce lavender-green pigment.

question: A patient with a human bite wound on the right forearm arrived at the clinic for treatment. The wound was inflicted 36 hours earlier, and a culture was taken by the physician on duty. After 48 hours, the culture results were: Gram-stain smear = Gram-negative straight, slender rods; Chocolate agar plate = 'Pitting' of the agar by small, yellow, opaque colonies; Oxidase = +; Motility = Neg; Glucose = +; Growth in increased CO2 = +; Catalase = Neg; Growth at 42°C = Neg. What is the most likely identification of this facultative anaerobe?

options.feedback: Incorrect. P. aeruginosa is catalase positive and grows at 42°C.
Incorrect. Acinetobacter is oxidase negative.
Incorrect. K. kingae produces β-hemolysis on sheep blood agar.
Correct! E. corrodens is part of the normal flora of the human mouth and typically 'pits' the agar. This organism is capnophilic (needing increased CO2).

question: A dog bite wound to the thumb of a 20-year-old male patient became infected. The culture grew a gram-negative, slender rod, which was a facultative anaerobe. The following results were noted: Oxidase = +; Catalase = +; Motility = Neg; Capnophilic = +; 'Gliding' on the agar was noted. What is the most likely identification?

options.feedback: Incorrect. P. aeruginosa is motile.

Correct! C. canimorsus is associated with septicemia or meningitis following dog bites. All Capnocytophaga strains are capnophilic, facultative anaerobic, gram-negative slender or filamentous rods with tapered ends.
Incorrect. Acinetobacter is oxidase negative.
Incorrect. Proteus is motile and oxidase negative.

question: A patient exhibits fever, chills, abdominal cramps, diarrhea, vomiting, and bloody stools 10 to 12 hours after eating. Which organisms will most likely grow from this patient's stool culture?

options.feedback: Incorrect. These typically have longer incubation periods.

options.text: E. coli O157:H7 or Shigella spp.
options.feedback: Correct! Both E. coli O157:H7 and Shigella spp. are invasive and cause bloody stools.
Incorrect. These typically cause vomiting without bloody stools.
Incorrect. Staphylococcus does not typically cause bloody stools.

question: When testing for coagulase properties, staphylococci isolates from a 67-year-old male diabetic patient showed a positive tube test (free coagulase). The organism should be identified as:

options.feedback: Correct! S. aureus is an opportunistic human pathogen. A wound or ulcer infected with S. aureus that is left untreated is especially detrimental to a diabetic patient.
Incorrect. S. haemolyticus is coagulase negative.
Incorrect. S. saprophyticus is coagulase negative.
Incorrect. Micrococcus is coagulase negative.

question: An isolate of Staphylococcus aureus was cultured from an ulcer obtained from the leg of a diabetic 79-year-old female patient. The organism showed resistance to methicillin. Additionally, this isolate should be tested for resistance or susceptibility to:

options.feedback: Incorrect. Erythromycin is not the primary concern for MRSA.
Incorrect. Gentamicin is not the primary concern for MRSA.
Correct! MRSA isolates are usually tested for susceptibility or resistance to vancomycin, a glycopeptide.
Incorrect. Kanamycin is not the primary concern for MRSA.

question: An isolate recovered from a vaginal culture obtained from a 25-year-old female patient who is 8 months pregnant is shown to be a gram-positive cocci, catalase negative, and β-hemolytic on blood agar. Which tests are needed for further identification?

options.feedback: Incorrect. These are for Streptococcus pneumoniae identification.

Correct! Group B streptococci (S. agalactiae) are important pathogens and can cause serious neonatal infections. Women who are found to be heavily colonized vaginally with S. agalactiae pose a threat to the newborn, especially within the first few days after delivery.
Incorrect. Methicillin is for staphylococci, not streptococci.
Incorrect. Coagulase is for staphylococci.

question: Which organism is the most often recovered gram positive cocci (catalase negative) from a series of blood cultures obtained from individuals with endocarditis?

options.feedback: Incorrect. S. agalactiae is not the most common cause of endocarditis.
Incorrect. C. perfringens is an anaerobic rod, not a catalase-negative coccus.
Correct! Enterococcus (Streptococcus) faecalis is the cause of up to 20% of the bacterial endocarditis cases and is the most commonly encountered species in this condition.
Incorrect. Pediococcus is not the most common cause of endocarditis.

question: A presumptive diagnosis of gonorrhea can be made from an exudate from a 20-year-old emergency department patient if which of the following criteria are present?

options.text: Smear of urethral exudate (male only) shows typical gram-negative, intracellular diplococci; growth of oxidase-positive, gram-negative diplococci on selective agar (modified Thayer–Martin)
options.feedback: Correct! N. gonorrhoeae can be presumptively identified from a male patient only from the Gram stain and growth on selective agar. In female patients, the normal flora from a urethral swab may appear to be N. gonorrhoeae but may be part of the normal flora.

options.text: Smear from vaginal area shows gram-negative diplococci; growth of typical colonies on blood agar
options.feedback: Incorrect. Blood agar is not selective enough and vaginal flora can mimic gonococci.

options.text: Smear from rectum shows typical gram-negative diplococci; no growth on chocolate agar
options.feedback: Incorrect. Rectal specimens are not reliable for presumptive diagnosis.

options.text: Growth of gram-negative cocci on MacConkey agar and blood agar
options.feedback: Incorrect. N. gonorrhoeae does not grow on MacConkey agar.

question: 'Clue cells' are seen on a smear of vaginal discharge obtained from an 18-year-old female emergency department patient. This finding, along with a fishy odor (amine) after the addition of 10% KOH, suggests bacterial vaginosis caused by which organism?

options.feedback: Incorrect. S. epidermidis does not cause bacterial vaginosis.
Incorrect. S. agalactiae does not cause bacterial vaginosis.

Correct! G. vaginalis, a gram-negative or gram-variable pleomorphic coccobacillus, causes bacterial vaginosis. 'Clue cells' are vaginal epithelial cells with gram-negative or gram-variable coccobacilli attached to them.
Incorrect. E. coli does not cause bacterial vaginosis.

question: A 1-month-old infant underwent a spinal tap to rule out bacterial meningitis. The CSF was cloudy, and the smear showed many pus cells and short gram-positive rods. After 18 hours, many colonies appeared on blood agar that resembled Streptococcus spp. or L. monocytogenes. Which of the following preliminary tests should be performed on the colonies to best differentiate L. monocytogenes from Streptococcus spp.?

options.feedback: Correct! L. monocytogenes is catalase positive and displays a 'tumbling' motility at room temperature. Streptococcus spp. are catalase negative and nonmotile.
Incorrect. These are for streptococcal identification, not for differentiating Listeria from Streptococcus.
Incorrect. Both are oxidase negative and glucose positive.
Incorrect. Coagulase is for staphylococci.

question: Acid-fast positive bacilli were recovered from the sputum of a 79-year-old man who had been treated for pneumonia. Which of the following test reactions after 3 weeks of incubation on Löwenstein–Jensen agar are consistent with Mycobacterium tuberculosis?

options.feedback: Correct! M. tuberculosis is niacin positive, nitrate reduction positive, and nonphotochromogenic. This organism takes up to 3 weeks to grow on selective agar.
Incorrect. Optochin is for Streptococcus pneumoniae, not mycobacteria.
Incorrect. PYR is for streptococci.
Incorrect. These are antibiotic susceptibility tests, not biochemical identification tests for mycobacteria.

question: Which biochemical tests should be performed in order to identify colorless colonies growing on MacConkey agar (swarming colonies on blood agar) from a catheterized urine specimen?

options.feedback: Correct! A swarmer on blood agar would most likely be a Proteus spp. A lactose nonfermenter and swarmer that is often isolated from urinary tract infections is P. mirabilis.
Incorrect. This is not the best set for Proteus identification.
Incorrect. Bile solubility is for Streptococcus pneumoniae.
Incorrect. This is not the best set for Proteus identification.

question: A gram-negative nonfermenter was isolated from a culture taken from a burn patient. Which of the following is the best choice of tests to differentiate Pseudomonas aeruginosa from Acinetobacter spp.?

options.text: Growth on MacConkey agar, catalase, growth at 37°C
options.feedback: Incorrect. Both grow on MacConkey, both are catalase positive.

Correct! P. aeruginosa has a distinctive grape odor. The best choice of tests is: P. aeruginosa is oxidase positive, motile, and grows at 42°C. Acinetobacter spp. are oxidase negative, nonmotile, and do not grow at 42°C.

options.text: Growth on blood agar, oxidase, growth at 35°C
options.feedback: Incorrect. Both grow on blood agar and at 35°C.
Incorrect. These tests are not used for Pseudomonas vs. Acinetobacter.

question: A Haemophilus spp., recovered from a throat culture obtained from a 59-year-old male patient undergoing chemotherapy, required hemin (X factor) and NAD (V factor) for growth. This species also hemolyzed horse erythrocytes on blood agar. What is the most likely species?

options.feedback: Incorrect. H. ducreyi requires X factor only.
Incorrect. H. parahaemolyticus requires V factor only.
Correct! H. haemolyticus requires both X and V factors for growth and lyses horse erythrocytes.
Incorrect. H. aegyptius requires both X and V but does not typically hemolyze horse erythrocytes.

question: Large gram-positive bacilli (boxcar shaped) were recovered from a blood culture taken from a 70-year-old female diabetic patient. The following results were recorded: Aerobic growth = Neg; Spores = Neg; Lecithinase = +; Anaerobic growth = +; Motility = Neg; Hemolysis = β (double zone); GLC (volatile acids) = acetic acid and butyric acid. What is the most likely identification?

options.feedback: Correct! C. perfringens is an anaerobic gram-positive rod that is often isolated from the tissue of patients with gas gangrene (myonecrosis). Spore production is not usually seen with this organism, which may also stain gram negative.
Incorrect. Fusobacterium are gram-negative rods.
Incorrect. Bacteroides are gram-negative rods.
Incorrect. C. sporogenes is motile and does not produce double zone hemolysis.

question: Anaerobic gram-negative rods were recovered from the blood of a patient after gallbladder surgery. The bacteria grew well on agar containing 20% bile, but were resistant to kanamycin and vancomycin. What is the most likely identification?

options.feedback: Incorrect. C. perfringens is gram-positive.

Correct! B. fragilis is the most often isolated gram-negative anaerobic bacillus. It is resistant to many antibiotics. A good screening agar is a 20% bile plate that does not support the growth of Prevotella spp. or Porphyromonas spp.
Incorrect. Prevotella do not grow well in 20% bile.
Incorrect. Porphyromonas do not grow well in 20% bile.

question: In Breakpoint Antimicrobial Drug Testing, interpretation of susceptible (S), intermediate (I), and resistant (R) refers to testing antibiotics by using:

options.text: The amount needed to cause bacteriostasis
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is not the definition of breakpoint testing.

options.text: Only the specific concentrations necessary to report S, I, or R
options.feedback: Correct! Breakpoint susceptibility testing is done by selecting only two appropriate drug concentrations for testing. If the results show growth at both concentrations, then resistance is indicated; growth only at the lower concentration signifies an intermediate result; no growth at either concentration is interpreted as susceptible.
Incorrect. This is a specific value, not the definition.

options.text: A dilution of drug that is one tube less than the toxic level
options.feedback: Incorrect. This is not the definition of breakpoint testing.

question: A CSF sample obtained from a 2-week old infant with suspected bacterial meningitis grew gram-negative rods on blood and chocolate agars. The following results were noted: MacConkey agar = No growth; Glucose (open) OF = +; Glucose (closed) OF = Neg; Indole = +; Motility = Neg; 42° C growth = Neg; ONPG = +; Urease = Neg; Catalase = +; Oxidase = +; Pigment = Yellow. What is the correct identification?

options.feedback: Incorrect. P. aeruginosa grows on MacConkey agar and is motile.
Correct! C. meningosepticum is a well-known cause of neonatal meningitis. It will grow well on chocolate agar, producing yellow pigmented colonies.
Incorrect. Acinetobacter is oxidase negative.
Incorrect. E. coli grows on MacConkey agar and is motile.

question: During the summer break, several middle-aged elementary school teachers from the same school district attended a 3-day seminar in Chicago. Upon returning home, three female teachers from the group were hospitalized with pneumonia, flulike symptoms, and a nonproductive cough. Routine testing of sputum samples revealed normal flora. Further testing using buffered CYE agar with L-cysteine and α-ketoglutarate in 5% CO2 produced growth of opaque colonies that stained faintly, showing thin gram-negative rods. What is the most likely identification?

options.feedback: Correct! L. pneumophila is the cause of pneumonia and can occur as part of an epidemic sporadically or nosocomially, or may be community acquired. The appearance of mottled, cut-glass colonies on buffered CYE agar under low power and the use of a direct immunofluorescence technique on sputum samples determine the presence of L. pneumophila.
Incorrect. H. influenzae does not require buffered CYE agar.
Incorrect. E. corrodens is not a cause of pneumonia.
Incorrect. S. pneumoniae would grow on blood agar and would not require CYE agar.

question: A vancomycin-resistant gram-positive coccobacillus resembling the Streptococcus viridans group was isolated from the blood of a 42-year-old female patient undergoing a bone marrow transplant. The PYR and leucine aminopeptidase (LAP) tests were negative. The following results were noted: Catalase = Neg; Esculin hydrolysis = Neg; Hippurate hydrolysis = Neg; CAMP = Neg; Gas from glucose = +; 6.5% salt broth = Neg. What is the correct identification?

options.feedback: Correct! Leuconostoc spp. are vancomycin-resistant opportunistic pathogens and follow invasive procedures. They are often recovered from positive neonatal blood cultures resulting from colonization during delivery.
Incorrect. Enterococcus is PYR positive and grows in 6.5% salt broth.
Incorrect. Staphylococcus is catalase positive.
Incorrect. Micrococcus is catalase positive.

question: A catalase-negative, gram-positive coccus resembling staphylococci (clusters on the Gram stained smear) was recovered from three different blood cultures obtained from a 60-year-old patient diagnosed with endocarditis. The following test results were noted: PYR = Neg; Esculin hydrolysis = Neg; Vancomycin = Sensitive; LAP = Neg (V); 6.5% Salt broth = Neg; CAMP test = Neg. What is the correct identification?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Leuconostoc are vancomycin resistant.
Correct! Gemella spp. are often recovered from patients with endocarditis and meningitis. On the Gram stain, they resemble staphylococci morphologically but are catalase negative.
Incorrect. Enterococcus is PYR positive.
Incorrect. Micrococcus is catalase positive.

question: An immunocompromised patient with prior antibiotic treatment grew aerobic gram-positive cocci from several clinical specimens that were cultured. The organism was vancomycin resistant and catalase negative. Additional testing proved negative for enterococci. What other groups of organisms might be responsible?

options.feedback: Correct! Leuconostoc spp. and Pediococcus spp. are vancomycin-resistant, catalase-negative, gram-positive aerobic organisms recovered from immunosuppressed patients.
Incorrect. These are vancomycin sensitive.
Incorrect. Micrococcus is catalase positive and Gemella is vancomycin sensitive.
Incorrect. Clostridium is anaerobic and S. bovis is vancomycin sensitive.

question: A catalase-positive, gram-positive coccus (clusters on Gram stain smear) grew pale yellow, creamy colonies on 5% sheep blood agar. The specimen was recovered from pustules on the face of a 5-year-old girl with impetigo. The following test reactions indicate which organism? Glucose = + (Fermentation); Oxidase = Neg; PYR = Neg; Bacitracin = Sensitive; Lysostaphin = Sensitive.

options.feedback: Incorrect. Micrococcus is bacitracin resistant and lysostaphin resistant.
Incorrect. Streptococcus is catalase negative.
Incorrect. Enterococcus is catalase negative.
Correct! S. aureus is a usual cause of skin infections and a common cause of cellulitis, impetigo, postsurgical wounds, and scalded skin syndrome in infants.

question: A wound (skin lesion) specimen obtained from a newborn grew predominantly β-hemolytic colonies of gram-positive cocci on 5% sheep blood agar. The newborn infant was covered with small skin eruptions that gave the appearance of a 'scalding of the skin.' The gram-positive cocci proved to be catalase positive. Which tests should follow for the appropriate identification?

options.feedback: Incorrect. These are for streptococci.

Correct! S. aureus is the cause of 'scalded skin' syndrome in newborn infants. The production of a potent exotoxin (exfoliatin) causes the epidermis to slough off, leaving the newborn's skin with a red, raw texture or a burned, scalded look.
Incorrect. These are for streptococci.
Incorrect. These are for streptococci.

question: A 20-year-old female patient entered the emergency clinic complaining of abdominal pain, fever, and a burning sensation during urination. An above-normal WBC count along with pus cells and bacteria in the urine specimen prompted the emergency physician to order a urine culture. The colony count reported for this patient revealed 100,000 col/mL of a nonhemolytic, catalase negative, gram-positive organism on 5% sheep blood agar. The following test results indicate which organism? PYR = +; 6.5% Salt broth = + growth; Bacitracin = Neg; Bile Esculin = +; Optochin = Neg.

options.feedback: Correct! E. faecalis gives a positive reaction to the PYR test and is often implicated in urinary tract infections (UTIs). It is part of the normal flora of the female genitourinary tract and the human gastrointestinal tract.
Incorrect. S. pyogenes is β-hemolytic and bacitracin susceptible.
Incorrect. S. agalactiae is PYR negative.
Incorrect. S. bovis is PYR negative and 6.5% salt negative.

question: A sputum specimen from an 89-year-old male patient with suspected bacterial pneumonia grew a predominance of gram-positive cocci displaying alpha-hemolysis on 5% sheep blood agar. The colonies appeared donut shaped and mucoidy and tested negative for catalase. The most appropriate tests for a final identification are:

options.feedback: Incorrect. These are for staphylococci.
Incorrect. These are for β-hemolytic streptococci.

Correct! S. pneumoniae colonies appear as α-hemolytic 'donut' shaped colonies on 5% sheep blood agar. To differentiate the viridans streptococci from S. pneumoniae, the most appropriate test is the Optochin disk test. S. pneumoniae on blood agar are susceptible to Optochin but viridans streptococci are resistant.
Incorrect. These are for group D and B streptococci.

question: A tissue biopsy specimen of the stomach was obtained from a 38-year-old male patient diagnosed with gastric ulcers. The specimen was transported immediately and processed for culture and histology. At 5 days, the culture produced colonies of gram-negative (curved) bacilli on chocolate and Brucella agar with 5% sheep blood. The cultures were held at 35°C–37°C in a microaerophilic atmosphere. The colonies tested positive for urease. The most likely identification is:

options.feedback: Incorrect. E. coli is not curved and does not require microaerophilic conditions.

Correct! H. pylori is not easily cultured for growth and identification. Tissue samples should be transported in appropriate media and tested immediately. Other means of successful identification are rapid urease test on biopsy material; urea breath test; and serological tests for the detection of antibodies to H. pylori.
Incorrect. E. faecalis is not a curved gram-negative rod.
Incorrect. S. bovis is a gram-positive coccus.

question: A catalase-positive, gram-positive short rod was recovered from the blood of a prenatal patient. The organism appeared on 5% sheep blood as white colonies surrounded by a small zone of beta-hemolysis. The following tests were performed, indicating the patient was infected with which organism? Motility = + (tumbling on wet prep) room temperature; Motility = + (umbrella-shape on semisolid agar) room temperature; Glucose = + (fermentation); Esculin = +; Voges–Proskauer = +.

options.feedback: Correct! Early detection in pregnant women is very important when dealing with L. monocytogenes. If it is not detected and treated, infection of the fetus, resulting in stillbirth, abortion, or premature birth may result.
Incorrect. S. agalactiae is catalase negative.
Incorrect. S. pyogenes is catalase negative.
Incorrect. Lactobacillus is not typically β-hemolytic and is not motile.

question: An emergency department physician suspected Corynebacterium diphtheriae when examining the sore throat of an exchange student from South America. What is the appropriate media for the culture of the nasopharyngeal swab obtained from the patient?

options.feedback: Incorrect. While Corynebacterium will grow on chocolate agar, Tinsdale agar is preferred.
Incorrect. Thayer-Martin is for Neisseria.

Correct! The diagnosis is made more rapidly when the examining physician alerts the laboratory that diphtheria is suspected, so that Tinsdale agar or other media containing tellurite salts can be used for culture. Tinsdale agar is the preferred culture medium because the potassium tellurite in the agar causes C. diphtheriae to produce brown colonies surrounded by a brown halo.
Incorrect. MacConkey agar inhibits gram-positive organisms.

question: A 25-year-old pregnant patient complained of vaginal irritation. Cultures taken for STDs proved negative. A Gram-stained vaginal smear revealed many epithelial cells with gram-variable short rods (coccobacilli) covering the margins. What is the most likely cause of the vaginosis?

options.feedback: Incorrect. Group B streptococci are gram-positive cocci.

Correct! G. vaginalis is part of the normal flora (anorectal) of adults and children. Clue cells (vaginal epithelial cells with gram-variable coccobacilli on the cell margins) are seen in vaginal washings.
Incorrect. S. aureus is a gram-positive coccus.
